perturbation.original_text: The University and/or Principal Investigator together with ArTara and the CRO will conduct the Project in accordance with the Project Plan and will use all reasonable endeavors consistent with their expertise to successfully complete the Project.
perturbation.changed_text: The University and/or Principal Investigator together with ArTara and the CRO will make efforts to conduct the Project, and will endeavor to complete the Project as they see fit.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will conduct the Project in accordance with the Project Plan' to 'will make efforts to conduct the Project' weakens the commitment to follow the plan. Replacing 'will use all reasonable endeavors consistent with their expertise to successfully complete the Project' with 'will endeavor to complete the Project as they see fit' introduces a subjective element, allowing for deviation from established protocols and standards.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR Part 50 Protection of Human Subjects
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 50.20(b) requires basic elements to be included in informed consent, which is essential for clinical trials. By allowing the parties to complete the project 'as they see fit,' there is a risk that essential elements of the Project Plan, including subject protections and proper informed consent, may be disregarded, potentially violating the requirement to obtain and document legally effective informed consent. This introduces ambiguity and conflicts with requirements of GCP.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: s? The FDA regulations do not preclude a member from being compensated for services rendered. Payment to IRB members should not be related to or dependent upon a favorable decision. Expenses, such as travel costs, may also be reimbursed. 9. What is the FDA role in IRB liability in malpractice suits? FDA regulations do not address the question of IRB or institutional liability in the case of malpractice suits. FDA does not have authority to limit liability of IRBs or their members. Compliance with FDA regulations may help minimize an IRB's exposure to liability. 10. Is the purpose of the IRB review of informed consent to protect the institution or the subject? The fundamental purpose of IRB review of informed consent is to assure that the rights and welfare of subjects are protected. A signed informed consent document is evidence that the document has been provided to a prospective subject (and presumably, explained) and that the subject has agreed to participate in the research. IRB review of informed consent documents also ensures that the institution has complied with applicable regulations. 11. Does an IRB or institution have to compensate subjects if injury occurs as a result of participation in a research study? Institutional policy, not FDA regulation, determines whether compensation and medical treatment(s) will be offered and the conditions that might be placed on subject eligibility for compensation or treatment(s). The FDA informed consent regulation on compensation [21 CFR 50.25(a)(6)] requires that, for research involving more than minimal risk, the subject must be told whether any compensation and any medical treatment(s) are available if injury occurs and, if so, what they are, or where further information may be obtained. Any statement that compensation is not offered must avoid waiving or appearing to waive any of the subject's rights or releasing or appearing to release the investigator, sponsor, or institution from liability for negligence [21 CFR 50.20]. II. IRB Membership 12. May a clinical investigator be an IRB member? Yes, however, the IRB regulations [21 CFR 56.107(e)] prohibit any member from participating in the IRB's initial or continuing review of any study in which the member has a conflicting interest, except to provide information requested by the IRB. When selecting IRB members, the potential for conflicts of interest should be considered. When members frequently have conflicts and must absent themselves from deliberation and abstain from voting, their contributions to the group review process may be diminished and could hinder the review procedure. Even greater disruptions may result if this person is chairperson of the IRB. 13. The IRB regulations require an IRB to have a diverse membership. May one member satisfy more than one membership category? Yes. For example, one member could be otherwise unaffiliated with the institution and have a primary concern in a non-scientific area. This individual would satisfy two of the membership requirements of the regulations. IRBs should strive, however, for a membership that has a diversity of representative capacities and disciplines. 1 14. When IRB members cannot attend a convened meeting, may they send someone from their department to vote for them? No. Alternates who are formally appointed and listed in the membership roster may substitute, but ad hoc substitutes are not permissible as members of an IRB. However, a member who is unable to be present at the convened meeting may participate by video-conference or conference telephone call, when the member has received a copy of the documents that are to be reviewed at the meeting. Such members may vote and be counted as part of the quorum. If allowed by IRB procedures, ad hoc substitutes may attend as consultants and gather information for the absent member, but they may not be counted toward the quorum or participate in either deliberation or voting with the board. The IRB may, of course, ask questions of
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces specific requirements for conducting a project with a more discretionary approach, potentially compromising subject protections and informed consent, which contradicts 21 CFR § 50.20(b), as explained in the Law Explanation. The modification weakens the obligation to follow the Project Plan and introduces a subjective element, potentially violating the requirement to obtain and document legally effective informed consent. This falls under the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The University and ArTara each agree to treat matters of authorship of the Publication in a proper collaborative spirit and following guidelines and policies in accordance with the University of Iowa's Operations Manual which may be found at: https://opsmanual.uiowa.edu/. It is anticipated that employees of the University will be first and senior authors on the Publication, but it is understood that final authorship will be determined in accordance with all applicable laws and regulations in publication practice, including Section 6002 of the Affordable Care Act a/k/a Sunshine Act and with ICMJE (International Committee of Medical Journal Editors) guidelines, standard scientific practice and journal guidelines.
perturbation.changed_text: The University and ArTara each agree to attempt to treat matters of authorship of the Publication collaboratively. It is anticipated that employees of the University may be considered for authorship, but final authorship will be determined at ArTara’s discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agree to treat' to 'agree to attempt to treat' weakens the commitment. Replacing the clear guidelines for authorship (ICMJE, Sunshine Act, standard practices) with 'at ArTara’s discretion' introduces ambiguity and the potential for authorship disputes or unethical authorship practices. This could violate regulations surrounding the reporting of clinical trial data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 42 U.S. Code § 282 - Requirements regarding the activities of the Director of NIH
perturbation.law_explanation: 42 U.S. Code § 282(j)(1)(C) addresses clinical trial registry data expansion, which includes ensuring that clinical trials are registered and results are reported to enhance transparency. If authorship is determined solely at ArTara's discretion, it can lead to biased publications or suppression of negative results, thus hindering complete and accurate reporting of clinical trial data. It contradicts the principles of transparency and public availability of clinical trial information.

perturbation.justification: The modification creates an ambiguity, undermining the original contract's commitment to collaborative authorship. The original text specifically references guidelines like the ICMJE and the Sunshine Act. The changed text weakens the commitment to authorship guidelines by stating that final authorship will be determined at ArTara's discretion, potentially leading to biased or suppressed results, conflicting with transparency and public availability principles as explained in the Law Explanation. 42 U.S. Code § 282 focuses on the Director of National Institutes of Health, but the Law Explanation connects it to ensuring clinical trial data transparency which, if authorship is solely at ArTara's discretion, undermines that transparency. The altered contract introduces an ambiguity that weakens adherence to ethical and regulatory authorship standards, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: ArTara, Affiliates, and sublicensees will obtain and maintain commercial general liability insurance with a reputable and financially secure insurance carrier prior to clinical testing, making, using, importing, offering to sell, or selling any licensed Product or engaging in any other act involving any licensed Product or the patent rights, if such act could possibly create risk of a claim against University Indemnitees for personal injury or property damage.
perturbation.changed_text: ArTara, Affiliates, and sublicensees will attempt to obtain commercial general liability insurance prior to clinical testing or selling any licensed Product, if deemed necessary.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will obtain and maintain' to 'will attempt to obtain' weakens the obligation to secure insurance. Adding 'if deemed necessary' introduces a subjective criterion for obtaining insurance, potentially leaving the University Indemnitees unprotected. This creates ambiguity around liability coverage.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Iowa Code § 669.14 - Authority to procure insurance
perturbation.law_explanation: Iowa Code § 669.14 grants state entities, including the University of Iowa, the authority to procure insurance to protect against liability. By making the insurance procurement discretionary ('if deemed necessary'), the altered text weakens the protection afforded to the University and potentially contradicts the principle that the university needs to be covered for the liabilities.

perturbation.justification: The original text states that ArTara 'will obtain and maintain' insurance, which is a clear obligation. The changed text weakens this to 'will attempt to obtain' and adds 'if deemed necessary,' making the insurance procurement discretionary. This contradicts the intent of Iowa Code § 669.14, which grants state entities, like the University of Iowa, the authority to procure insurance to protect against liability, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. The change introduces ambiguity around liability coverage, potentially leaving the University Indemnitees unprotected. The first scraped law snippet, while not directly referencing insurance requirements, indicates a complex document, suggesting the existence of more detailed requirements that this change could violate by making insurance optional.
